AOEr5FrW-lY.title: We Were Almost Entirely Wrong About China
AOEr5FrW-lY.transcript:
0:00 We had a theory about China. We had read
0:02 things, watched the news, talked to
0:05 people who had been there. We thought we
0:07 had a reasonable picture of what to
0:09 expect.
0:10 We were wrong. Not a little wrong,
0:13 almost entirely wrong. This is the first
0:16 video in a series about 22 days inside
0:19 China.
0:20 We traveled privately, just the two of
0:23 us, with a different local guide in each
0:25 city.
0:27 No tour buses, no group schedules, just
0:30 us moving through one of the most
0:33 extraordinary countries on earth trying
0:35 our best to understand it. We are going
0:38 to show you the landscapes and the food
0:41 and the things you come to China to see.
0:45 But we are also going to try to explain
0:47 something harder. What this country
0:49 actually is, how it thinks, what it has
0:52 been through, why it ended up here.
0:56 Because if you only watch the pretty
0:57 pictures, you will miss the whole story.
1:00 My name is Tresa. That guy you keep
1:03 seeing on screen exploring everything,
1:06 sitting in every car, that's my husband,
1:09 Zarko.
1:11 Together, we run this channel and
1:13 together we spent 22 days traveling
1:16 China privately.
1:18 We came back with more questions than
1:20 answers, which honestly is the best
1:22 possible outcome from any trip.
1:25 I'm American, born and raised in the US,
1:29 which means I grew up with a pretty
1:30 specific picture of the world.
1:33 Communism was the enemy. The Soviet
1:35 block was gray and scary.
1:38 And China was this distant, mysterious
1:41 place you only heard about when
1:43 something went wrong.
1:45 Zarko's story is different. He grew up
1:48 in Bulgaria under communism. Not reading
1:51 about it, living it. one party, state
1:54 control over everything. You kept your
1:56 head down and you did not ask the wrong
1:59 questions.
2:01 He left at 25 as a political refugee,
2:04 built a business in Seattle that
2:06 supported 125 families. And when we
2:09 retired, we moved back to Bulgaria to
2:13 Burgus on the Black Sea. That was 7
2:16 years ago.
2:17 I'm telling you all of this because it
2:19 matters for everything you're about to
2:21 see. When I look at China, I'm looking
2:24 through American eyes. When Zarko looks
2:27 at China, he's looking through the eyes
2:29 of someone who actually lived inside a
2:31 communist system.
2:33 Our questions are completely different.
2:35 And when we compare notes at the end of
2:37 the day, neither one of us has the full
2:40 picture alone, but together we get a
2:42 little closer to something real. That's
2:45 what this series is about. Getting
2:48 closer to something real about China.
2:50 Before we left, we spent weeks trying to
2:53 prepare,
2:55 reading, watching documentaries,
2:57 talking to anyone who'd actually been
2:59 there. And we kept coming back to the
3:02 same question. How
3:05 how does a country running a communist
3:07 system, the same basic system that gave
3:10 Bulgaria empty shelves,
3:12 that gave the Soviet Union the goologs,
3:15 that gave the world North Korea?
3:18 How does that same system produce the
3:20 world's second largest economy? Bullet
3:23 trains, cashless cities, more new
3:26 billionaires every year than anywhere
3:28 else on the planet. It didn't add up.
3:31 And for Zarko, who actually lived inside
3:33 that system, it made even less sense
3:36 than it did for me. So, either China was
3:39 doing something very different from what
3:40 the textbooks say, or the textbooks were
3:43 missing something important. We booked
3:46 22 days to find out.
3:49 We flew out of Istanbul. Turkish
3:51 Airlines is practically on our doorstep
3:53 from Burgus. And Istanbul has become one
3:56 of the great travel hubs in the world,
3:58 connected to almost everywhere. With
4:01 business class fairs that are a fraction
4:03 of what you'd pay on a European carrier.
4:06 On a 10-hour flight, business class
4:08 stops being a luxury. It becomes
4:11 self-preservation.
4:12 We landed early morning at Shanghai
4:14 Puong. And for our very first experience
4:17 of China, we decided to take the magv
4:20 into the city. Magnetic levitation, no
4:24 wheels on the track, top speed of 430
4:27 kmh.
4:29 It felt like exactly the right way to
4:31 arrive.
4:32 Honest review, it's 20 years old. The

4:38 in Germany, built here, then largely
4:41 abandoned worldwide because it was too
4:43 expensive and too inflexible to scale.
4:47 The train was nearly empty on a weekday
4:49 morning. The interior felt dated, and
4:52 the top speed we actually hit was around
4:55 300 km hour, which sounds impressive
4:58 until you learn that China's regular
5:01 high-speed trains now reach similar
5:03 speeds across the entire country. We'd
5:06 see that for ourselves on our first
5:08 bullet train ride a few days later. Our
5:11 hotel was right on Nanjing Road, not
5:13 near it, on it. We opened the curtains
5:16 and there it was, directly below us. The
5:19 most famous commercial street in
5:21 Shanghai, running from the bun straight
5:24 through the heart of the city. It never
5:26 stops, day or night. We slept for a few
5:30 hours. The 7-hour time difference from

5:36 walking.
5:38 This is the moment, the one I'd heard
5:40 other travelers talk about but hadn't
5:42 quite believed.
5:44 when what you expected and what you're
5:47 actually standing inside quietly come
5:50 apart.
5:52 The streets are spotless. The
5:54 architecture is extraordinary.
5:56 Every luxury brand you've ever heard of
5:58 is here
6:01 alongside Chinese brands I'd never
6:03 encountered.
6:06 People are well-dressed, unhurried,
6:09 going about their lives. Everything
6:11 moves with this purposeful efficiency. I
6:14 can only describe as organized.
6:16 People compare Nanjing Road at night to
6:19 Time Square. I've been to Time Square.
6:22 Time Square is loud and chaotic in a way
6:25 that feels almost accidental.
6:27 Nanjing Road feels deliberate, like
6:30 someone asked what a great city street

6:34 the answer.
6:36 There's a robot that makes coffee right

6:48 We walked to the Bund as the sun was

7:20 families, young couples, older people in

7:31 China has become a massive internal

7:40 >> The next morning, we had something

8:28 Smartphones, tablets, laptops,

9:02 Then the screen unfolds into a fulls

9:07 Something between a tablet and a

9:18 Zarko stood at that table for a long
9:20 time.
9:23 Then the Xiaomi store. Xiaomi makes
9:26 everything. Phones, TVs, rice cookers,
9:30 scooters, robot vacuums,
9:34 and a car. The Xiaomi SU7 is an electric
9:37 sedan that's been making European and

9:44 It looks better than most of what it
9:45 competes with,
9:47 and it costs considerably less.
9:51 the DJI store. Everything you're
9:54 watching right now was filmed on a DJI
9:56 Osmo Pocket 4. Zarko's camera of choice
9:59 for this channel. Walking into the DJI
10:02 flagship felt a little ceremonial.
10:05 Walking store to store that morning,
10:07 something kept pulling at me. This isn't
10:10 innovation arriving from nowhere. China
10:12 graduates more engineers every year than
10:15 any other country on Earth. The
10:18 government identified AI, electric
10:20 vehicles, and advanced manufacturing as
10:23 national priorities and then sustained
10:26 that investment for decades. Whatever
10:29 you think of the political system behind
10:30 those decisions, the output is sitting
10:33 right in front of you and it works.
10:36 >> You can go 1,000 km on this 1,400
10:40 hybrid.
10:42 One of the most remarkable things we saw
10:44 in Shanghai wasn't a landmark or a tech
10:47 showroom. It was a supermarket. Hearesh,
10:50 owned by Alibaba, looks like an upscale
10:53 grocery at first. Seafood tanks where
10:56 you point at your fish and they prepare

11:02 food section, normal aisles. But look
11:05 up. Running above the entire store is a

11:10 ceiling tracks with bags hanging from
11:12 them, moving in every direction. A
11:15 silent river of orders flowing overhead

11:28 Employees walk the aisles filling online
11:30 orders in real time. bag them, hook them

11:44 We were told some people specifically
11:46 choose where to live based on whether

11:58 the financial district at lunchtime, we
12:00 stop to watch the office lunch system in

12:24 a drone arrived. an actual drone

12:32 landing precisely on a marked drop zone.
12:35 The food came from a restaurant on the

12:46 Our guide told us her mother, an elderly

12:53 groceries, meals, household items. She

13:00 arrives at her door. She never has to
13:02 carry heavy bags or fight through

13:20 China, it runs it remarkably well.
13:24 We also spent time in the BYD showroom.
13:27 Build your dreams.
13:29 Now the largest electric vehicle
13:31 manufacturer in the world by sales.
13:33 Bigger than Tesla.
13:35 Bigger than Volkswagen.
13:37 In China, electric vehicles aren't a

14:22 On our last morning in Shanghai, our

14:26 expecting.

14:30 showroom, she took us to a park. It was

14:50 >> What is the name of this park?
14:51 >> Pushing Park.
14:52 >> Pushing Park. Pushing Park.
14:53 >> Pushing Park.

15:01 >> Wow.
15:04 >> Tai Chi in one corner. a ballroom
15:07 dancing group nearby. 20 people partner

15:18 Further along, a group of musicians

15:26 And then further still, a group just
15:29 singing together like they did this

15:38 deceptively easy, slow, balanced,

15:49 one foot, moving through those
15:51 sequences, it takes real muscle, real

16:06 We spent almost two hours there. We

16:31 air, moving, playing, singing,

16:35 Zaro said something afterwards that I
16:37 keep coming back to. He said, "This is
16:40 what retirement should look like." I
16:43 couldn't argue with him. After those
16:45 first few days in Shanghai, one question
16:48 kept coming back. We'd walked Nanjing
16:50 Road, stood at the Bund, watched a drone
16:54 deliver lunch in 12 minutes, danced with
16:57 strangers in a park at 8:00 in the
16:59 morning. How did this happen? Here's
17:01 what I knew going in. In 1950, China was
17:05 one of the poorest countries on Earth.
17:08 The average person here earned less than
17:10 the average person in subsaharan Africa.
17:13 A century of war, invasion, famine, and
17:17 political upheaval had torn the country
17:19 apart. No electricity in most villages,
17:23 no paved roads, no real industry. And
17:26 then it got worse before it got better.
17:29 In 1958, Mao Zaong launched what he
17:32 called the Great Leap Forward. The plan
17:35 was to transform China into a modern
17:37 industrial power. Within 15 years,
17:41 private farming was abolished.
17:44 The people at home had nothing left to
17:46 eat. Between 15 and 45 million people
17:50 died in the famine that followed.
17:54 This is the China that existed within
17:56 living memory.
17:58 Many of the older people we met on this
18:00 trip, guides, hotel staff, people who
18:04 looked roughly our age, were born into
18:06 that world, or their parents were. Real
18:09 hollow stomach starvation isn't distant
18:12 history here. It's one generation back.
18:15 Then in 1978, something changed. Mao had
18:20 died two years earlier. A man named Dung
18:22 Xiaoing took power. And Dung looked at
18:25 the wreckage of the previous 30 years
18:28 and made a decision that was remarkable
18:30 for how simple it was. Whatever works,
18:33 he said, if a cat catches mice, it
18:36 doesn't matter if it's black or white.
18:38 He opened China to foreign investment,
18:41 allowed private business alongside the
18:43 state, created special zones where
18:45 market capitalism was explicitly
18:48 permitted just to test it, to see what
18:51 happened. He sent Chinese students
18:53 abroad to study in western universities.
18:56 He rewarded results. He kept the
18:58 communist party in political control,
19:01 kept the one party system, the state
19:03 surveillance, the control of
19:05 information. But onto all of that he
19:08 grafted a form of capitalism so
19:10 aggressive and competitive. It makes
19:13 most western economies look cautious.
19:16 And the result, China today is not
19:19 communist in any way Markx would
19:21 recognize. It's something with no clean
19:24 name. A one party political structure
19:27 running on top of an extraordinarily
19:29 dynamic market economy.
19:31 The government sets direction. The
19:33 market does the work. The people get
19:36 richer and in exchange for that rising
19:38 prosperity, they accept a level of state
19:41 control over their political lives and
19:44 their access to information that would
19:46 be unthinkable in a western democracy.
19:50 From Shanghai, we flew south to Guin, a
19:53 landscape so ancient it's been appearing
19:55 in Chinese paintings for 2,000 years. We
19:59 cruised the Lee River. We climbed into
20:01 the rice terraces of Lji
20:04 and we started to understand that China
20:06 is not one thing. It's many things at
20:09 once and they don't always agree with
20:11 each other. Over the next videos, we'll
20:14 take you through 11 cities and 22 days.
20:19 Landscapes unlike anything in Europe.
20:22 People who were warmer and more generous
20:24 than either of us had any reason to
20:27 expect.
20:30 And we'll try to understand without
20:32 looking away from the complicated parts
20:35 what China actually is.

M7UL58ZUwPg.title: Bulgaria Switches to Euro: The Night Money Changed Forever
M7UL58ZUwPg.transcript:
0:00 The clock is ticking on a century of
0:02 history. Bulgaria is saying goodbye to
0:05 the lit. It's financial revolution
0:08 happening right before our eyes. Banks
0:11 are resetting, ATMs are flashing, and an
0:15 entire nation is holding its breath.
